values: Moderate

evidence.quote_or_summary: Moderate

values: Suitable for Fit Beginners

evidence.quote_or_summary: Suitable for Fit Beginners

values: Uneven trails with daily ascents and descents
Continuous steep ascent from Sepi to Rammam village
Continuous 10 km steep ascent from Samanden to Molley with 4,460 ft height gain
Steep descent from Sandakphu to Gurdum that can get slippery and tricky in rain or snow
Walking on an exposed ridge from Phalut to Sandakphu

evidence.quote_or_summary: Every day will include ascents and descents on uneven trails.
The route from Sepi to Rammam village is a continuous steep ascent that will test anyone’s limit.
The trail to Molley from Samanden is a continuous 10 km steep ascent with 4,460 ft of height gain.
The descent from Sandakphu to Gurdum is steep, and it can get slippery and tricky when it's raining or snowing.
On the days you trek from Molley to Phalut and Phalut to Aahl, it gets extremely cold. This is because you are walking on an exposed ridge throughout.

values: Sepi to Rammam: continuous steep ascent
Samanden to Molley: continuous 10 km steep ascent with 4,460 ft height gain
Sandakphu to Gurdum: steep descent that can be slippery in rain or snow
Day 3: steepest climb from 7,760 ft to 11,220 ft (4,460 ft height gain)

evidence.quote_or_summary: The route from Sepi to Rammam village is a continuous steep ascent that will test anyone’s limit.
The trail to Molley from Samanden is a continuous 10 km steep ascent with 4,460 ft of height gain.
The descent from Sandakphu to Gurdum is steep, and it can get slippery and tricky when it's raining or snowing.
The steepest climb is on Day 3 taking you from 7,760 ft to 11,220 ft, giving you a 4,460 ft height gain.

values: Indiahikes provides microspikes, gaiters, helmets, ropes, and other technical gear wherever required
Hydration recommended to avoid muscle cramps on steep ascents

evidence.quote_or_summary: our Trek Leaders and Technical Guides will provide you with all necessary guidance, support, and technical gear such as microspikes, gaiters, helmets, ropes, etc. wherever required.
Hydrate well to avoid muscle cramps.

values: Trek reaches up to 11,930 ft with total gain of ~5,530 ft
Day 3 climb from 7,760 ft to 11,220 ft (4,460 ft gain) can contribute to altitude sickness
Risk of Acute Mountain Sickness (AMS) above 10,000 ft; can affect first-time and experienced trekkers alike
Even fit trekkers climbing too high too soon are at equal risk of AMS
Indiahikes team carries high-altitude medical kits, oxygen cylinders, and stretchers

evidence.quote_or_summary: you will gain approximately 5,530 ft (6,400 ft to 11,930 ft)

The steepest climb is on Day 3 taking you from 7,760 ft to 11,220 ft, giving you a 4,460 ft height gain. This quick climb along with the exertion of the trek and exposure to the cold can lead to the development of altitude sickness in some people.
At altitudes above 10,000 ft, the chances of being hit by Acute Mountain Sickness are real. Altitude sickness can happen to anybody - first-time trekkers as well as experienced trekkers.
even a fit person climbing too high too soon runs an equal risk of developing AMS.
The Indiahikes team is equipped with high-altitude medical kits, oxygen cylinders, and stretchers to handle any altitude-related emergencies.

values: Extremely windy and cold on the ridge from Phalut to Sandakphu, especially in autumn and winter
Snowfall possible near Phalut, especially in winter or towards end of January
Strong winds at Phalut significantly amplify the chill factor
Prolonged rain can make trails slushy and unsafe to walk
Snowfall can obstruct previously made paths, requiring fresh tracks to be cut
Trek may be aborted or turned back from any campsite if weather deteriorates

evidence.quote_or_summary: you are walking on a ridge throughout from Phalut to Sandakphu. This makes it extremely windy and cold especially in autumn and winter.
snowfall is a distinct possibility near Phalut. Especially in the winter months or towards the end of January.
Phalut is famous for its strong winds, which can significantly amplify the chill factor.
Rain can make the trails too slushy to walk safely on. Snowfall can obstruct previously made paths, requiring fresh tracks to be cut on the snow.
If the weather deteriorates, the team may need to turn back from any campsite to ensure the safety of all trekkers.

values: Closest exit between Sepi and Molley is via the roadhead at Rammam
After Molley, closest exit is via the motorable road ahead of Sabagram connecting to Maneybhanjyang
Closest hospital on the trek is at Rimbik (before Sandakphu)
After Sandakphu, closest hospital is in Sukhia Pokhri (drive via Mane Bhanjang)
For serious medical attention, head to Siliguri for better facilities
Indiahikes team carries high-altitude medical kits, oxygen cylinders, and stretchers
Trekkers must inform Trek Leader of any pre-existing medical conditions

evidence.quote_or_summary: If you are on the route between Sepi and Molley, the closest exit would be via the roadhead at Rammam.
After crossing Molley, the closest exit would be via the motorable road ahead of Sabagram, that connects Sandakphu-Phalut to Maneybhanjyang and beyond.
the closest hospital on the trek is at Rimbik (before reaching Sandakphu). After you cross Sandakphu, the closest hospital is in Sukhia Pokhri.
If you need serious medical attention, it is best to head down to Siliguri for better facilities.
The Indiahikes team is equipped with high-altitude medical kits, oxygen cylinders, and stretchers to handle any altitude-related emergencies.
Please inform your Trek Leader about any pre-existing medical conditions or health concerns, no matter how minor they may seem.

values: Safety is a shared responsibility; trekkers must actively follow safety protocols
Trekkers should disclose any pre-existing medical conditions to the Trek Leader
Weather monitoring by technical team may lead to turning back from any campsite

evidence.quote_or_summary: we believe that safety is a shared responsibility. We need your active participation and attention to safety protocols to ensure a safe and successful trek.
Please inform your Trek Leader about any pre-existing medical conditions or health concerns, no matter how minor they may seem.
Our technical team, in conjunction with the Trek Leader, constantly monitors the weather conditions and makes informed decisions about proceeding with or aborting the trek.
